questions.question: How much can naming and description quality alone move tool-selection accuracy?
options: Under 1 percentage point — selection is dominated by model size
10-20 percentage points on benchmarks; one 50-tool registry went from 62% to 89% after a description rewrite
It only matters for registries with over 1,000 tools
Accuracy is fixed by the provider and cannot be influenced
questions.explanation: Composio, Anthropic, and Databricks all report 10-20 point swings purely from renaming and rewriting descriptions. Description quality is the cheapest reliability lever you have.

questions.question: What are the two characteristic failures of tool selection in a large registry?
options: Slow calls and expensive calls
Wrong tool picked (ambiguous descriptions) and no tool picked when one fits (the model hallucinates an answer instead)
Schema errors and network errors
Tools calling each other in loops
questions.explanation: When two descriptions both say "look up people," the model cannot disambiguate; when a description says "retrieve financial data" but the user says "stock price," the model may answer with a hallucinated number instead of calling the tool.

questions.question: What is the two-sentence description pattern that consistently improves selection accuracy?
options: "This tool is very useful. Please use it often."
"Use when {condition}. Do not use for {close-but-wrong cases}."
"Input: {schema}. Output: {schema}."
"WARNING: read fully before calling. See documentation."
questions.explanation: The "Use when X. Do not use for Y." pattern works because the negative line disambiguates against close-competitor tools in the registry — the main source of wrong-tool picks. Keep it under 1024 characters (OpenAI truncates beyond that in strict mode).

questions.question: Why are atomic tools (notes_list, notes_create, notes_delete) preferred over one monolithic do_everything(action, options) tool?
options: Atomic tools run in parallel; monolithic ones cannot
Models select by name with typed schemas instead of parsing an action string — monolithic tools bench 15-30% worse
Monolithic tools exceed provider size limits
Atomic tools are cheaper per call
questions.explanation: An action string plus untyped options dict is the worst possible selection surface. Rule of thumb: if the action argument has more than three values, split the tool.

questions.question: Why should tool error messages be written for the model rather than for a developer log?
options: Providers reject stack traces in tool results
The error reaches the model as context — a teaching error like "Invalid input: 'city' is required. Example: {...}" cuts retry counts in half
Models cannot read Python exceptions
Short errors save tokens, which is the only benefit
questions.explanation: Tool errors are injected back into the conversation, so they are prompts. "TypeError: NoneType has no attribute 'lower'" teaches nothing; a typed error with an example shows the model exactly how to correct the call.
